perturbation.original_text: The Employer will not take any action in dealing with the Insurer that would impair any right or interest of the Employee in the Policies.
perturbation.changed_text: The Employer will endeavor not to take actions that unduly impair the Employee's rights or interests in the Policies.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly prohibits the employer from impairing the employee's rights. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'endeavor not to' and 'unduly impair,' making the employer's obligation less strict and open to interpretation. 'Endeavor not to' implies an effort but not a guarantee, and 'unduly impair' introduces a subjective standard.
perturbation.contradicted_law: ERISA - Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974
perturbation.law_explanation: ERISA requires fiduciaries to act 'solely in the interest of the participants and beneficiaries' and 'with the care, skill, prudence, and diligence...that a prudent man...would use.' By weakening the obligation to protect the employee's rights with vague terms like 'endeavor not to' and 'unduly impair,' the modified text introduces a standard that could fall short of ERISA's fiduciary duty, potentially allowing actions that harm the employee's interests if they are not deemed 'undue' or if the employer merely 'endeavors' to avoid them. This could lead to a breach of fiduciary duty under ERISA.

Fiduciary duties (a) Prudent man standard of care (1) Subject to sections 1103(c) and (d), 1342, and 1344 of this title, a fiduciary shall discharge his duties with respect to a plan solely in the interest of the participants and beneficiaries and- (A) for the exclusive purpose of: (i) providing benefits to participants and their beneficiarie
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens the employer's obligation to protect the employee's rights. The original text states the employer 'will not take any action' that would impair the employee's rights, while the changed text states the employer will 'endeavor not to take actions that unduly impair' the employee's rights. This contradicts the fiduciary duties required by ERISA, which mandates acting 'solely in the interest of the participants and beneficiaries'. The phrase 'endeavor not to' implies effort without guarantee and 'unduly impair' introduces a subjective standard, potentially allowing actions that harm the employee's interests. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'fiduciary shall discharge his duties with respect to a plan solely in the interest of the participants and beneficiaries'. The change introduces ambiguity that could fall short of this fiduciary duty, which is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any income or employment taxes due for a year on the value of current life insurance coverage provided to the Employee under this Agreement will be the responsibility of the Employee. To the extent that the value of current life insurance coverage is taxable income, it will be subject to withholding requirements and will be reported as income by the Employer to the IRS and state-taxing authorities on the Employee's W-2 Form or other IRS or state-required reporting form to the extent required by law.
perturbation.changed_text: Any income or employment taxes due for a year on the value of current life insurance coverage provided to the Employee under this Agreement will generally be the responsibility of the Employee. To the extent that the value of current life insurance coverage is taxable income, it may be subject to withholding requirements and may be reported as income by the Employer to the IRS and state-taxing authorities on the Employee's W-2 Form or other IRS or state-required reporting form.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that taxes are the employee's responsibility and that the employer 'will' report the income. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'generally' and 'may be', making the tax responsibility less absolute and the reporting obligation discretionary. This weakens the enforceability and clarity of the tax obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: IRC Section 3402 mandates that employers withhold income tax from wages. By changing 'will be subject to withholding requirements' to 'may be subject to withholding requirements', the modified text suggests that the employer has discretion in whether or not to withhold, directly contradicting the mandatory withholding requirement of the IRC. Similarly, the use of 'may be reported' instead of 'will be reported' undermines the employer's mandatory reporting obligations to the IRS.

Any tables or procedures prescribed under
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes 'will be subject to withholding requirements' to 'may be subject to withholding requirements' and 'will be reported' to 'may be reported.' According to 26 USC 3402, 'every employer making payment of wages shall deduct and withhold upon such wages a tax.' The use of 'may' introduces discretion, contradicting the mandatory withholding requirement stipulated in the law. The original text aligns with the employer's obligation to withhold and report, while the changed text undermines this legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Within 60 days after the death of the Employee, the Employer will provide to the "Insurer" a written statement indicating the amount of each policy's proceeds the Employee's beneficiary is entitled to receive.
perturbation.changed_text: Within a reasonable timeframe after notification of the Employee's death, the Employer will attempt to provide the "Insurer" with an estimate of the amount each policy's proceeds the Employee's beneficiary may be entitled to receive.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear deadline (60 days) and obligation (will provide). The modified text replaces '60 days' with 'reasonable timeframe,' which is vague and subjective. It also changes 'will provide' to 'will attempt to provide an estimate' and 'is entitled to receive' to 'may be entitled to receive,' weakening the obligation and introducing uncertainty about the accuracy and certainty of the statement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: ERISA - Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974
perturbation.law_explanation: ERISA Section 503 requires that a plan administrator provide adequate notice in writing to any participant or beneficiary whose claim for benefits is denied, setting forth the specific reasons for denial and providing a full and fair review procedure. By changing the requirement to provide a statement of the amount the beneficiary is entitled to receive to 'an estimate of the amount each policy's proceeds the Employee's beneficiary may be entitled to receive', the modified language introduces uncertainty and weakens the beneficiary's ability to clearly understand their entitlement and challenge any discrepancies. The shift from a definite statement of entitlement to a potentially inaccurate estimate undermines the beneficiary's ability to make informed decisions regarding their claim and appeal rights under ERISA.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 29 CFR § 2560.503-1 - Claims procedure. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 29 CFR § 2560.503-1 - Claims procedure. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 2560.503-1 Claims procedure. (a) Scope and purpose. In accordance with the authority of sections 503 and 505 of the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 ( ERISA or the Act), 29 U.S.C. 1133 , 1135 , this section sets forth minimum requirements for employee benefit plan procedures pertaining to claims for benefits by participants and beneficiaries (hereinafter referred to as claimants). Except as otherwise specifically provided in this section, these requirements apply to every employee benefit plan described in section 4(a) and not exempted under section 4(b) of the Act. (b) Obligation to establish and maintain reasonable claims procedures. Every employee benefit plan shall establish and maintain reasonable procedures governing the filing of benefit claims, notification of benefit determinations, and appeal of adverse benefit determinations (hereinafter collectively referred to as claims procedures). The claims procedures for a plan will be deemed to be reasonable only if— (1) The claims procedures comply with the requirements of paragraphs (c), (d), (e), (f), (g), (h), (i), and (j) of this section, as appropriate, except to the extent that the claims procedures are deemed to comply with some or all of such provisions pursuant to paragraph (b)(6) of this section; (2) A description of all claims procedures (including, in the case of a group health plan within the meaning of paragraph (m)(6) of this section, any procedures for obtaining prior approval as a prerequisite for obtaining a benefit, such as preauthorization procedures or utilization review procedures) and the applicable time frames is included as part of a summary plan description meeting the requirements of 29 CFR 2520.102-3 ; (3) The claims procedures do not contain any provision, and are not administered in a way, that unduly inhibits or hampers the initiation or processing of claims for benefits. For example, a provision or practice that requires payment of a fee or costs as a condition to making a claim or to appealing an adverse benefit determination would be considered to unduly inhibit the initiation and processing of claims for benefits. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to provide a definitive statement of the policy proceeds. ERISA Section 503, as supported by 29 USC 1133 and 29 CFR § 2560.503-1, requires adequate notice in writing to beneficiaries regarding their claim for benefits. The original contract mandated a 'written statement indicating the amount of each policy's proceeds the Employee's beneficiary is entitled to receive,' while the changed text uses 'an estimate of the amount each policy's proceeds the Employee's beneficiary may be entitled to receive.' This weakens the guarantee of an accurate statement and undermines the beneficiary's ability to understand their entitlement, contradicting the purpose of ERISA.
